a:r.a:t: The authorization subsystem implements Role-Based Access Control, or RBAC, which is the industry standard for managing who can do what in an enterprise system. Let me walk through the model. Principals are the identities - users and service accounts, each with a unique ID. Roles are named permission bundles like admin, editor, and viewer. Bindings map a principal to a role on a specific resource - so you might bind user Alice to the editor role on collection "hr-docs". Memberships handle group-level role inheritance so you do not have to assign roles individually to every user. And Permissions are the granular resource-action pairs like "collection:read" or "agent:invoke". The system exposes 15 admin endpoints for managing all of this - CRUD operations for principals, roles, bindings, memberships, plus a permissions query and an effective-access endpoint that resolves the full permission set for a given principal. The authz service in authz/service.py is about 269 lines and provides tenant-scoped resource access. It integrates with the API gateway via gateway_security.py, with connector endpoints via connector_security.py, and with the skill runtime for per-skill access filtering. Tool policy enforcement happens in tools/policy.py. This is the foundation - the next evolution is ABAC, attribute-based access control, which would add dataset-level and column-level checks.
